topic: A homeowner learns their house was a former crime scene
raw_output:
[story 1]

The realtor, a cheerful woman named Brenda, had been impossibly upbeat, practically vibrating with enthusiasm as she pointed out the 'character' of the old Victorian. I’d been eager to sell, eager to move on, but as I walked through the drawing-room, the air thickened. A strange, metallic tang hung in the stale, dusty atmosphere. My gaze snagged on the patterned wallpaper near the fireplace. It wasn't just old; there were faint, almost invisible scuff marks embedded in the paper, patterns that didn't look like wear and tear. A cold prickle traced its way down my spine, starting in my neck and spreading rapidly through my limbs. I backed away slowly, my breath catching in my throat, the cheerful facade of the house suddenly feeling like a grotesque mask. I wanted to bolt, to run out into the bright afternoon sun, but my feet seemed rooted to the spot, my eyes unable to leave those unsettling marks.

[story 2]

I spent the first week unpacking boxes, trying to impose normalcy on the unsettling quiet of the old house. It was the silence that gnawed at me. Every floorboard creak sounded amplified, every distant siren seemed too close. One evening, while cleaning out the basement, I found a loose floorboard under the old pantry. Prying it up revealed not dirt, but a dark, dried stain. It was irregular, smeared, and undeniably something that had been violently disturbed. My hands began to shake so badly I had to grip the edge of the workbench to steady myself. I stared at the stain, my mouth going dry. A wave of profound unease washed over me, a physical pressure building behind my sternum. I felt a desperate urge to cover the discovery, to bury it under layers of mundane junk, but the image of what that stain represented refused to leave my vision.

[story 3]

The inspector had been vague, mentioning 'previous occupancy issues' and 'unusual structural anomalies.' I dismissed it as old house quirks. Then, I started noticing the details. In the hallway closet, tucked behind a stack of moth-eaten blankets, I found a small, tarnished silver locket. When I opened it, there was no picture inside, only a smear of dark residue on the velvet lining. A chill settled deep in my core, making the hairs on my arms stand rigid. I stood there for a long time, the silence of the house pressing in, suffocating. My heart hammered against my ribs, a frantic, uneven rhythm. I started pacing the perimeter of the room, my movements jerky and uncontrolled, as if I were trying to outrun an unseen presence that seemed to cling to the very plaster of the walls.
